exemplar.title: To what extent does household income in Taman Bukit Margosa influence preferences for substituting plant-based proteins for beef, considering greenhouse gas emissions?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does household income in Taman Bukit Margosa influence preferences for substituting plant-based proteins for beef, considering greenhouse gas emissions?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report follows a logical sequence with clear headings
weaknesses: Structure is unevenâlarge raw-data tables break flow
Formatting inconsistencies and typos reduce readability
Repetitive and verbose sections hinder concision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and organization, terminology/concision, and logic/coherence each align with the middle band (band 2). Although the report is generally logical and uses correct terminology, sustained organization and concision are lacking. Thus, 2/3 accurately reflects the communication quality.

strengths: Clear, focused research question naming independent and dependent variables
Comprehensive discussion of global and local environmental context
Coherent explanation of links between income, dietary choice and emissions
weaknesses: Some connections remain asserted rather than fully evidenced
Context section is verbose and contains repetition
Research question wording is slightly wordy
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place the research question and issue context at the top of the 3â4 band and into the 5â6 band respectively. The student formulates a coherent, focused question (band 5), and situates it within globalâtoâlocal GHG context effectively (band 5), though some links are not fully evidenced. The connection strand was competent (band 4). On balance, this criterion merits 5/6 for strong focus and context with minor omissions.

strengths: Methodology is repeatable with clearly described survey sections and ANOVA plan
Awareness of ethical considerations such as confidentiality and pilot testing
weaknesses: Sampling strategy is described but not justified or procedurally detailed
Ethical and risk protocols lack consent procedures and dataâstorage details
Link between survey questions and variables is not always explicit
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design sits in the upper 3â4 band (band 4) but sampling strategy and ethical considerations lack justification and depth (band 3 each). Given these uneven performances and significant omissions in sampling and ethics, a holistic score of 3/6 reflects a competent but underâjustified plan.

strengths: Identifies method strengths and weaknesses linked to quantitative approach
Suggests meaningful modifications (larger sample, focus groups)
Briefly evaluates conclusion in the environmental context
weaknesses: Evaluation of conclusion lacks discussion of uncertainty and confidence intervals
Method evaluation omits non-response and self-selection biases
Improvement suggestions are not prioritised or operationally detailed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall within the 3â4 band at the lower level (band 3): conclusions, method evaluation and improvements are present and meaningful but incomplete. This consistent performance across strands yields a 3/6 score reflecting a competent yet under-developed evaluation.

strengths: Data analysis is correct and interprets p-values with respect to hypotheses
Appropriate use of summary statistics (means, SD) and ANOVA testing
Construction of diagrams/charts covers most relevant data
weaknesses: Figures and tables often lack complete labels, units and legends
ANOVA tables are summarised without full statistics (df, F-values)
Missing checks for analysis assumptions and effectâsize reporting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation fits band 3â4 (appropriate but with omissions), analysis is correct but incomplete (band 4), and conclusions are supported yet lack effectâsize and interval metrics (band 4). Overall, the work merits 4/6 for sound analysis and presentation with notable gaps in labelling and statistical reporting.

strengths: Application proposal is clearly justified based on findings with international precedent
Solution proposal relates directly to the investigationâs emissions data
weaknesses: Evaluation of strengths and weaknesses is partial and lacks depth on feasibility, cost and monitoring
Discussion of solution limitations is not comprehensive
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The proposal strand meets the top descriptor (band 3) but the evaluation of strengths/weaknesses sits at band 2. Balancing a full application proposal with a limited evaluation results in an overall mark of 2/3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Terms such as ANOVA, p-value, independent/dependent variable are used correctly, but the report is verbose and sometimes colloquial (âIt shows thatâ¦â).  Either appropriate terminology or concision is achieved, not both, matching band 2.

The student comments on how cultural attitudes might override income effects and references an external U.S. study, linking findings back to GHG emissions.  Yet the evaluation is brief and does not fully explore implications or uncertainties of the statistical result, leaving noticeable omissions.

The conclusion restates that income does not significantly influence substitution preferences and cites p-values to support this.  It also recognises that expectations were not met.  However, confidence intervals and effect sizes are absent, so support is adequate rather than exhaustive, positioning the work at the upper end of band 3-4.

Means, standard deviations and an ANOVA test are calculated correctly and interpreted; the student explains p-values and links them to hypothesis testing.  Nevertheless, no check for normality or homogeneity is shown, and only a single ANOVA table is provided despite multiple variables.  The analysis is therefore correct but incomplete, earning 4.

The student proposes increasing sample size and adding focus groups/interviews to address depth and biasâmodifications that are directly linked to cited weaknesses.  However, they do not prioritise which change would most improve validity nor specify how focus-group data would be integrated, so suggestions are meaningful but not yet significant.

The methodology section specifies survey delivery, three sections, Likert scaling, and planned ANOVA testing.  A full question list and scoring plan are provided, making replication feasible.  Nonetheless, the link between questions and the stated variables is not always explicit, and no pilot-verified validity statistics are shown, so sufficiency rather than complete appropriateness is evident.  This aligns with the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Connections between beef-related emissions, income-based dietary choices and the chosen question are described (e.g., wealthier households meeting fruit-and-vegetable targets, expected knowledge differences).  However, some links remain asserted rather than fully evidenced, and the explanation of why income, rather than culture or age, is singled out is not entirely convincing.  This places the strand in the competent 3-4 band; the presence of several omissions justifies 4 rather than 5.

The student formulates a clear, single-sentence question that names the independent (household income) and dependent (preference for plant-based protein instead of beef) variables, specifies the study location (Taman Bukit Margosa) and ties the investigation to greenhouse-gas emissions.  The wording is sufficiently narrow to be answerable with the proposed survey and statistics, showing both relevance and focus.  Minor wordiness (âconsidering greenhouse gas emissionsâ) does not reduce coherence, hence top-band quality but with slight lack of elegance, warranting 5 rather than 6.

The discussion notes strengths (financial incentives, reduced emissions) and a weakness (potential conflict with exporting countries and unemployment issues), but does not explore feasibility, cost or monitoring challenges in depth.  Only some relevant pros and cons are evaluated, aligning with 2 marks.

Headings and a logical sequence (Issue â Method â Data â Conclusion) are provided, yet extensive raw-data tables break flow and some tables repeat.  Organisation is present but not sustained consistently, fulfilling the middle band.

Strengths (quantitative comparability, Likert ease) and weaknesses (leading questions, small sample) are identified, and the impact of low reliability is explained.  Limitations such as self-selection bias or non-response are not analysed, so the discussion remains descriptive rather than exhaustive.

The student discusses global food-production emissions, quotes percentages for livestock contributions, and links them to flooding, heat waves and local beef consumption.  The explanation moves from global context to the situation in Negeri Sembilan and the selected housing estate.  Although the analysis is occasionally repetitive, it clearly situates the investigation within a recognisable environmental issue, demonstrating depth rather than a simple outline.

The student claims to use random sampling and classifies households into B40/M40/T20, but provides no procedure for generating the random list or ensuring representativeness.  While the rationale that âeach resident has an equal chanceâ is stated, it is not justified with a concrete mechanism.  Thus the choice is described but not justified, meriting the lower mark within band 3-4.

Privacy and confidentiality are acknowledged (Diagram 2), and a pilot test is mentioned, indicating awareness of ethics and some risk mitigation.  However, physical risks, data-handling procedures and consent protocols are not systematically detailed.  The considerations are outlined rather than comprehensively described, placing the work solidly at 3 within the 3-4 band.

The report contains raw-data tables, bar charts and pie charts for most survey items.  Several figures, however, are poorly labelled (axes missing units, colour keys incomplete) and some relevant data such as ANOVA tables are only summarised.  Hence diagrams are appropriate but contain omissions, matching the middle of the 3-4 band.

Introducing an Emissions Trading Scheme for Malaysian livestock is clearly derived from the findings about beef emissions and is supported with the New Zealand precedent (Diagram 10).  The student explains subsidies and methane inhibitors, providing justification based on environmental and economic arguments, meeting the top descriptor for this criterion.

Most arguments follow a logical order, but frequent typographical symbols in the raw data and abrupt jumps between figures require rereading.  Overall coherence exists, yet parts are difficult to follow, so band 2 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The environmental issue section is verbose and repeats similar points; refine to eliminate redundancy, focus on key GHG statistics and their relevance to the research context.
Diagram 1âs axes lack units and the colour key is missing; include clear labels and legends so readers can interpret COâ values per serving correctly.
The research question wording is somewhat wordy and could be streamlined to improve clarity and concision.
The justification for using Google Forms to reach âdiverse racesâ is superficial; you should explain how this platform supports sampling validity and reduces selection bias.
Table 1âs description of random sampling lacks procedural detail; specify how the random list was generated (e.g. random number generator, census list) to justify representativeness.
Ethical considerations mention confidentiality but omit consent protocols and data storage procedures; outline how informed consent is obtained and data secured.
Diagram 2 lists privacy measures but lacks detail on how consent and data anonymization were operationalized; include procedures and documentation.
The Likertâindex description is overly detailed and repetitive; consider summarizing scale choices concisely to improve readability.
The raw data in Table 2 uses inconsistent math fonts and symbols (bold, fraktur, overlines) which obscure the values; standardize formatting for clarity.
The transition from raw data tables to summary statistics lacks narrative explanation; add a brief description of data cleaning or transformation steps.
Several figures are unlabeled or embedded without axes labels, making trends difficult to follow; ensure all graphs include titles, axes, units, and legends.
Table 3(c) contains placeholder symbols â~â and â\ddotscâ which suggest incomplete processing; replace with actual values or remove interim markers.
The processedâdata Table 4 lacks context on how percentages were calculated; include formulas or a brief note on the denominator and rounding approach.
Table captions are inconsistently placed and occasionally miss âFigureâ/âTableâ identifiers; maintain uniform caption style and positioning.
The mean formula $$\bar{x}=\frac{\sum fx}{n}$$ is presented without defining f (frequency) or x values; add definitions to ensure reproducibility.
The standard deviation formula is given but no discussion of assumptions (normality, homogeneity); you should mention checks or limitations.
Interpretation of Diagram 5 implies causal inference (âstronger knowledge causes behaviourâ); reframe to avoid over-statement as the data are observational.
Table 6âs heading mislabels response section as âMean and standard deviationâ but the table in Section C covers frequencies; revise to reflect content.
The term âIOUSEHOLDâ in Table 5 is a typo and should be corrected to âHOUSEHOLDâ for a professional presentation.
The hypothesis statements are generic; specify expected direction of difference (e.g. higher income correlates with higher substitution preference) for sharper testing.
ANOVA results in Table 9 are reported without degrees of freedom or F-values; include full ANOVA table (df, SS, MS, F) for transparency.
The conclusion evaluation briefly notes cultural factors but does not address uncertainty or confidence intervals; expand to discuss these limitations.
Method evaluation notes small sample but omits discussion of non-response bias and how it could affect trends; include these limitations in the evaluation.
The proposed ETS application lacks analysis of implementation challenges (costs, enforcement, equity); evaluate these strengths and weaknesses.
Bibliography entries vary in style (some include date first, others author first); adopt one consistent citation format throughout.
